tokens: We don't really...I'd like to describe for a LM window description.
We had so much practice at that, I'd like to see how I could do.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'll take (meaning "listen to") any words you've got.
We expended all our questions a few minutes ago with John, and in fact I didn't even have to ask any; he just answered them all.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Looking out at 12 o'clock on the horizon, there is a very hilly, subdued region - well, let's say hilly terrain - at 12 o'clock that goes on out of view around to 11.
It's rolling with white pockmarked craters there, and I'd say that's maybe 50 to 100 meters above the surrounding terrain where we are.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Where again was this boulder with the fillet?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: It's at about 2:30, maybe a couple of hundred meters out, and it's on this side of the ridge that trends east-west here, that blocks out Smoky.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Could it be sliding down the ridge, and that's why the fillet's on the south?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I was just going to say, it's downslope so that might have been what happened.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Though the slope doesn't appear that steep, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Boy, that's really...(Stops to listen) We sure saw it on descent.
I don't see it right now.
There's a bright crater to the right, maybe 50 meters of what I think is Spook, which is probably Buster, but I really wouldn't swear to it.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Can you tell (meaning "see") boulders over there?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: There's not a one, as far as I can see.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You're right up to your old peak.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Coming down on...(Listens) Okay, coming down, Tony, on descent it looks...As John has described...There's a distinct ray pattern across our landing site from South Ray.
And the boulders get...effectively (thin out and) disappear by (the time) we get to Palmetto.
And then they don't reappear again until almost the flank of North Ray.
(On the "Descartes EVA-I, III 2 of 2" map and the corresponding contour map, which is Figure
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You were mentioning the boulders and the rays from South Ray.
The ray itself, could you map out what extent it was, or was it just the whole general area?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: It was a pretty wide ray coming across here.
I would say it goes from our position perhaps to Spook.
And maybe behind us maybe another 100 meters or so.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: How about left-right extent, did it go all the way back to South Ray?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Well, you'll have to ask John that.
I couldn't see out that way.
As we...The biggest block that I saw was one we flew over which is maybe 100 meters to 200 meters behind us, and it looked like Volkswagen size.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: He'll be back up in a little bit, and I'm going to start the chow.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And, Tony, I wouldn't give you 2 cents for that orange juice as a hair tonic; it mats it down completely.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, that might be the point.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: We'd like to go your Suit Gas Return (valve) back to Cabin.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: We also tried this in orbit and we got the same sound, Tony.
(Pause) Okay, it gives us the same sound.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, we'd like to go back to Egress.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I tell you what it is.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'd like to leave you that way.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Have you guys got a suggestion of what meal you want us to eat?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Ready to answer Charlie's meal question) Okay.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I guess we'd like you to just go ahead with your first lunar meal.
I guess that deserves some champagne, I don't know.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Well, like John said earlier, we're definitely not going to get scurvy; we've got so much orange drink here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We're going to eat day 5, meal B.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Houston, John finally found his spoon.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I can't see how far the rays...I just assumed that this is a block field we're in from South Ray.
It goes about 100 meters out at 10 o'clock and goes over a ridge and disappears.
The next time I see it, is at South Ray, which is, you know, pretty far away from here (about 6 km).
South Ray is a doggone interesting crater.
I wish we could get to it.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The boulders on the west rim of it (meaning South Ray Crater) are just thick and white as they can be, and in the middle of it - you know, on your map where it looks like it's a depression - there appears to be a brown...(Having some trouble expressing himself) A sort of a gray, patch of dirt or something that was thrown out of that side of it.
And then on the north, there's another ray of very white boulders coming out of it.
Of course, we could see the ray pattern long before pitchover.
At 22,000, I was able to get my nose up against the window and see...The clue to where we were was South Ray.

Because at 22,000 and at 60-degree pitch angle, we couldn't even see Stone Mountain or any of the things in the rear (that is, features east of the landing site), but you just didn't have any doubt in your mind with that big crater.
And the way the pattern went, you work your around the pattern...I used the same gouges (meaning indicators or landmarks) to find out where we were going to land that we use on the L&A.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: On the...North Ray is pure white; South Ray is pure white; and it blends into a gray (as you move away from either crater); and then over here by us, it's almost totally gray.
I guess you just get the feeling that these rocks (around the LM) may have come from somewhere else (rather than from the immediate landing area).

There's a big subangular rock that I see at 10 o'clock - no, at 11 o'clock at about 100 meters (out from the LM) - that I would sure like to go over and look, because it looks like it's just one big piece of whatever rock it is.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Oh, and I do happen to see a white clast in the bottom of it.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I was wondering about the albedo on your surface chart, on the strips and things, whether the rays are as obvious as they are on the high-Sun-angle chart that you're carrying, or whether they look very much different at your low Sun angle.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: No, they're not, I don't think.
I can see from here down to
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: But there are some strange looking craters over there on Stone Mountain (see, for example AS16-113-
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I was just wondering about whether you could recognize whether you're on a ray by albedo as well as the boulder content.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I think you're going to be able to; but, boy, you're not going to pick up a contact; it just tails out into something.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Outstanding, that's better than I thought.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: You ought to be able to work across the contact.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: But you mainly would do it (that is, tell if you were on a ray) by the white boulders in the ray, I think.
I can see, on ridge lines, from here, I can see three different rays out of South Ray, I believe.
(We'll) have to go down there and look at them to make sure.
They seem to be riding on the ridge lines, although that's probably deceptive because I can't see down in the holes.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Tony, one other comment from my side.
Distances are pretty deceiving here for me.
I'm looking out over John's shoulder, and it looks like to me you could throw a rock into South Ray from our present position - which is, I know, impossible.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: A second comment has to do with the orbital, since we got so much (of a) look at the ground sailing around (for three extra orbits) waiting to come down.

Everywhere we saw the ground - which is just about the whole sunlit side - in the crater walls and on the ridges, we had the same lineation that the Apollo 15 photography showed on Hadley Delta and Hadley Mountain (see, for example, AS15-90-
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: And I'm looking out here at Stone Mountain, and I got a picture of it (earlier).
And it looks like it's got...Looks like somebody has been out there plowing across the side of it.
The benches just look like one sort of terrace right after another, right up the side.
And they sort of follow the contour of it right around.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: No, Tony, not that I can tell from here.
Those terraces could be rays (of ejecta) out of Stubby or something like that.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You mentioned two different rock types.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I can see (that) Stubby has...Right at the edge of Stone,
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (Helping John verbalize the thought) You think Stubby is punched into the edge (of Stone Mountain)...
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Garbled under Tony) Do y'all have a next...(Listens)
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, that's my guess from here but there again, the thing is so steep that the whole side of Stone Mountain, right now, from...A good half of it is in shadow.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: One thing; you mentioned two rock types: the black and white ones and then the all white ones.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Yeah, there was one right out in front of the LM here, just to the right of the footpad that looks like a breccia to me, Tony.
Either that or an indurated regolith (which is soil compacted in an impact).
We'll tell you when we get out.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Tony, we'll give you an analogy of what that black and white rock looks like.
It's really a gray and white and looks like a granitic rock with very large crystals to it, though I kind of doubt that (it is granitic).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: There are really some interesting rocks out here.
I see some that are pure snow white.
And we've got the whole run of them.
It's hard to tell at this sunlight, which is so bright on the surface, just exactly what color these things are, even with the naked eye.
I swear I see one out there with some pink in it, (chuckles) but we'd better wait until we get out.
We'll pick it up and make sure.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: What do you call tomato soup made with cold water, Tony?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: When you get a chance, could you take a look at that ridge at 12 o'clock, which you described as 50 to 100 meters out, and see if that continues on around to 10 and 9?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: It continues on around to my side.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: John's original observation was that it looked like we're in a big old subdued crater, and that's really what it looks like, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Man, those black and white rocks really look interesting, Tony.
I just can't wait to grab one of those.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I tell you, Charlie, we feel the same way.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: In fact, the impression you get is that it almost looks like the color of labradorite.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Tony, I guess it's really a bluish cast instead of real black to me.
But in this Sun it looks bluish.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Well, we'll bring you a small one of each.
I'll tell you one thing, I'm glad we brought the rake, because we really can do it.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We can get a rake sample (that is, a bagful) out in front of the lunar module with one scoop.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And when you get a chance, we'd like you to stow those hoses.
I guess we don't have enough friction in there, and the water separators are running wild.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: And if you can pull yourselves away from the window there, we'd like you to hold to the schedule of starting pre-sleep in about 20 minutes.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: How can we start pre-sleep in 20 minutes when we haven't even gotten to eat yet, Tony?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (Pause) Hey, the back room gave you a bravo on your descriptions there.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I'm like a little kid on Christmas Eve, Tony.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Boy) It really is neat to have a gravity field around to set stuff on.
That is really the cat's meow.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The hoses are hooked back up, Tony.
You should see some decrease in the separator (speed).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Pause) I think I know how you feel, Charlie.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Tony, how is (Command Module) Casper doing?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You've been keeping me so occupied here, but they've got no problems.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Boy, you can't imagine how nice this one-sixth gravity is.
This is the first time I've been able to eat soup without knowing whether I was going to eat it or take a bath in it.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And, Tony, John and I'd just like to give our thanks to the backroom guys and everybody that worked so hard on Casper's problem, giving us a chance to get here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I think everybody around here appreciates their job.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Gee, I'm sure glad somebody was able to come to that conclusion, because it sure looked black there for a while, didn't it?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'd like to get somebody to put in the word to the Big Sim Troop in the sky and tell Him to let's make it a little more nominal from here on out.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: (Pause) That was too much like a Sim.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Would you verify that the O2 demand Regs are in Cabin?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: It looked like the pressure was dropping down a little bit.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: And while you're eating there, I might brief you on a couple of things.
At about 108 hours, which is about time you'll be going to bed there, the RCS pressure will build up to the point where you'll get an RCS light again.
And just reset; there's nothing to worry about.
And then sometime just before you wake up in the morning, you may very well get a second caution light and alarm when the helium thrust pressure gets built back up to 1700.
And if you go to helium monitor on the Temp/Press gauge there, that'll go away.
(Pause) There's no way we can inhibit that.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: In other words, we're going to wake up twice tonight already, huh?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: The first one should go off before you get to bed, though.
But that second one will probably come on just before you should wake up.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: (Pause) How much sleep from the time when we start to bed do you want us to get?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We're just wondering why you're not using the Gatorade?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: The stuff we filled from the Command Module this morning we drank.
And that leaves us with two bags for two subsequent EVAs.
And we could fill one of the other bags and just drink water on the third or whatever you want us to do.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Yeah, I think we'd rather save the fortified stuff 'til the last.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: What's our GET (Ground Elapsed Time) right now?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Who do you want on biomed tonight?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's what I was afraid of.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: You've been looking at me since landing so we'll just stay right here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Pause) Okay, while you're worrying about that, your comm configuration for the night will be S-Band Power Amplifier, Secondary, as present.
The Telemetry will be Low; Voice will be Down Voice Backup, Range will be Off and, as I said, you're on biomed.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, it's S-Band Power Amplifier, Secondary, as present; Telemetry, Low; Voice will be Down-Voice Backup; Range, Off; and you're on biomed.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, Tony, we have Down-Voice Backup, biomed right, Telemetry, Low.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, you're pretty weak there for a second Charlie.
(Static remains, but the volume and readability improves)
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, at your convenience, I have some changes to your emergency lift-off checklist in the Surface Checklist.
Just give me a call when you're ready to take it.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, Tony, I'm ready to copy, if you'll give me a page.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, it's in the Surface Checklist, (page) 11-1.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, on the PGNS activation; it's down in the bottom left-hand side.
The last entry there is "Pro and hold until Standby light off." Cross that line out.
(Pause) And add the line underneath "CB or circuit breaker panel 11 (
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, on the second column, they have a correction to the checklist, it says "Circuit 16"...(Correcting himself) "Circuit breaker panel 16 (CB(16)) and Inverter 1, close." That should read "Circuit breaker panel 11 Inverter 1, close." Then we'd like to insert...
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, and we'd like to insert, underneath that line, "Circuit breaker panel 16, Inverter 2, Close" and "Inverter switch to 2".
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, Inverter to 2, and cross out Inverter 1.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay on (page) 11-2, underneath...(Correcting himself) On the left-hand, upper side you have "Asterisk CB 11 and 16".
Underneath that line write in "T-ephemeris update, if available for MSFN." (Pause)
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, is that an up-link or do we load?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: John thinks it's Verb 25 Noun 07 Enter, 1706 Enter, and then load the ephemeris, and I think that's correct.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: On your circuit breaker configuration here, I've got some that will be open and you might as well note that they will be open and that's okay.
Panel 11, first line there, S-band antenna will be open.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: And on 11-4, fourth line, panel 16, S-band antenna will be open.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Okay, and we go on to 11-6 now.
You have the setup for your steerable antenna and you can just cross all that out; and that's the end of it.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: We copy all of those updates.
The only one I don't understand is on 11-1, on the PGNS activation.
We crossed out the Pro and added an LGC (LM Guidance Computer)/DSKY closed and right before that it says LGC/DSKY closed.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: That's an error on our part.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: I just thought maybe something went by me, there.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, Tony, if that's everything, we're ready to go to bed.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: (Long Pause) Okay, we're through with everything here, and we're all set to let you go to bed.
You're going to bed, I'll have you know, a whole 6 minutes early.
I guess the government can allow you to have that time off.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: John will be off comm, and we're going to turn off the lights now.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We'll see you tomorrow, and we're sure looking forward to it.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: You turn all the lights off and it doesn't get dark (because of light leaks around the window shade).
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, Tony, we'd like to thank everybody for the great job of regrouping, and getting back to what seems to be pretty nominal from now on.
And we'll see you in the morning.
I guess you can give us a reveille call over the squawk box here.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I'll come in and I'll whistle something here.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Well, I guess I can't stop talking.

One final observation, Tony, is that due to the lack of dust that we had on landing and the fact that we can see blocks embedded in the side of these craters, here, I kind of got the distinct impression that the regolith is not too thick around here.
And we ought to maybe think about where would be the thickest place in order to get the drill in.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: From the films you've seen of other descents, do you think the dust was less than any of the others?
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Well, John will have to really comment on that.
But as far as my side goes, the little I looked out, there was by far...We could see, or, I could, on my side, see right on down through it to touchdown.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Okay, and from listening to your descent, it sounded like you picked it up about 90 feet.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: It was a little bit less than that.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: I have a feeling you and I could just sit up all night and talk about this.
transcript.speaker: England

tokens: Well, that's all you're going to hear from me.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: Tony, you guys missed it by about 10 minutes.
The RCS Reg A light just came on.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Hey, you know, a bit ago we had a little MA-RCS problem ....
now shows a 10 to 15 percent quantity.
Is that what you guys were expecting?
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: If y'all are happy, we'll see you in a couple of hours and go back to sleep.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: If you guys are happy, we'll go back to sleep.
transcript.speaker: Duke

tokens: We've got one circuit breaker we want you to open.
[Pause] Rendezvous Radar, Operate, panel 11, row 3, under Heaters.
transcript.speaker: Peterson
